programs.name: Elderly and Persons with Physical Disabilities (EPD) Waiver Program

eligibility.income_limits: Countable income must not exceed 300% of SSI (e.g., $2,743/month in 2023, $2,382/month in 2021, $2,130/month in 2013) or meet Medicaid spend-down; no household size table specified, applies to individual[2][3][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: $4,000 countable assets for individual (e.g., checking/savings/investments); home equity ≤$1,130,000 in 2026 if living in home or intent to return (exempt if spouse or dependent child in home); exemptions include primary home under equity limit, spouse/dependent child home occupancy[1][2][3][4]
other_requirements: Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) via interRAI Home Care Assessment (≥9 points on ADLs, cognition, behavior)
DC Medicaid provider-completed Prescription Order Form (POF) signed by physician/APRN
Liberty Healthcare face-to-face assessment for level of need
Live in home, family home, or assisted living (no room/board coverage)

benefits.value: Long-term care services/supports in home or assisted living to avoid nursing home: personal care aide, adult day health, home health aide, Services My Way (family/friend paid caregiving); specific hours/dollars not fixed, based on assessed level of need[2][3][4][7]

methods: Contact Aging and Disability Resource Center (ADRC) via dacl.dc.gov/service/epd-waiver or Department of Aging and Community Living (DACL) Medicaid Enrollment Services Unit (MES)
Phone: 1-800-677-1116 (Elder Locator) or local Department of Aging[7]
application.processing_time: ESA determines eligibility within 45 calendar days after complete application submission[3]
application.waitlist: Currently has a waiting list on first-come, first-served basis[5]

gotchas: Must be Medicaid-eligible (or become so via spend-down), but apply for waiver first[7]
POF must be fully completed by DC Medicaid provider; incomplete forms delay processing[3]
NFLOC not automatic (e.g., dementia diagnosis alone insufficient; needs ≥9 points on assessment)[1]
Home equity limit applies ($1,130,000 in 2026); countable assets strictly $4,000[1][4]
Waitlist is first-come, first-served; no priority bypassing mentioned[5]
No room/board in assisted living; services only[4]
programs.data_shape_notes: Income at 300% SSI (updates annually, no household table); fixed $4,000 individual asset limit with home equity cap; services tiered by assessed NFLOC level; waitlisted with statewide first-come processing

programs.name: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMB) Program

eligibility.income_limits: In the District of Columbia, eligibility requires household income up to 300% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL) plus a $20 disregard. For 2026 (approximate based on available data): 1-person household: $3,990 + $20 = $4,010 monthly; 2-person household: $5,410 + $20 = $5,430 monthly. Limits scale by household size per FPL tables, with DC applying higher threshold than federal standard[1][2].
eligibility.asset_limits: DC does not impose asset limits for QMB eligibility, aligning with states that eliminated resource tests[1][4].
other_requirements: Entitled to Medicare Part A or Part B (or both)
U.S. citizen or eligible immigration status

benefits.value: Pays Medicare Part A and Part B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments for covered services. Also provides Extra Help for Medicare Part D, limiting prescription costs to a few dollars each. Providers cannot bill QMB enrollees for Medicare cost-sharing; payments are considered full[1][2][5][6][7].

methods: Phone: 202-727-8370 (DC State Health Insurance Assistance Program/SHIP) or contact DHCF
In-person or mail: Contact DC Department of Health Care Finance (DHCF) for locations and addresses
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; automatic enrollment required in DC starting October 1, 2024, for eligible individuals[4].
forms: Specific form not named; apply through DHCF Medicaid application process
documents: Proof of U.S. citizenship or immigration status

offices_or_providers: Administered uniformly by DC Department of Health Care Finance (DHCF) across District

gotchas: DC uses higher 300% FPL limit vs. federal 100% FPL standard, but confirm current FPL figures as they update annually[1][2]
No asset test in DC, unlike many states[4]
QMB Plus (Full Duals) provides full Medicaid + Medicare help; distinguish from QMB-only[1][9]
Providers cannot bill you for Medicare cost-sharing, even if out-of-state[6]
Must already have Medicare Part A; automatic enrollment may apply post-2024[3][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: DC-specific: 300% FPL income threshold (higher than federal), no asset limits, uniform statewide administration, integrates with Full Duals/QMB Plus for broader benefits

programs.name: DC State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; prioritizes people with limited incomes but open to all Medicare beneficiaries, families, and caregivers[1][3]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests apply[1]
other_requirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary, family member, or caregiver living in the District of Columbia[1][3]

benefits.value: Free one-on-one personalized health insurance counseling, education, and assistance on Medicare (Parts A, B, C, D), Medigap, Medicaid, Medicare Savings Programs, Extra Help/Low Income Subsidy, federal retiree benefits, durable medical equipment, billing issues; group educational presentations, outreach at community events, and Senior Medicare Patrol services to prevent fraud[1][3][4]

methods: In-person: DC Dept. of Aging and Community Living, 250 E Street SW, Washington DC 20024 (hours 9:00 am - 5:00 pm)[2][4]
application.processing_time: No formal application or processing time; services provided directly upon contact[1][3][4]
forms: No specific forms required; counselors assist directly[1][4]
documents: Medicare card, current insurance details, billing statements if applicable (recommended but not mandatory)[4]

offices_or_providers: Primary office: DC Dept. of Aging and Community Living (DACL), 250 E Street SW, Washington DC 20024; delivered statewide via partnerships with local agencies[1][2]

gotchas: Not a financial aid or healthcare provider program—only free counseling and education, no direct payments or medical services; services are unbiased and do not sell plans; prioritizes limited-income and dually eligible but open to all; increasing demand may affect availability despite no waitlist[1][3][6]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income or asset test; counseling-only service for Medicare beneficiaries; single centralized DC provider via DACL with phone/email/in-person access

programs.name: DACL Senior Wellness Centers

eligibility.income_limits: No specific income limits identified; open to DC residents age 60+ without mention of financial thresholds in program descriptions[3][5][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits specified for Senior Wellness Centers[3][5][6]

benefits.value: Comprehensive programs promoting health and wellness, including health activities, social activities, nutrition services, trips, and events; typically available Monday-Friday 8:00 AM-5:00 PM (varies slightly by center); programming offered virtually and in-person[3][5]

methods: Contact specific center directly by phone to sign up for activities and trips (e.g., Bernice Fonteneau: (202) 727-0338; Model Cities: (202) 635-1900; Hayes: (202) 727-0357; Congress Heights: (202) 563-7225); Call DACL Information and Referral Assistance (I&R/A) line for general intake and screening; Visit centers in-person or check event calendars on center websites[2][5]
application.processing_time: No formal processing time; direct sign-up for activities, no enrollment or re-certification required for trips and programs[2]
application.waitlist: No waitlist mentioned; expansions at some centers (e.g., Model Cities, Congress Heights) to increase capacity[2][3]
forms: No specific forms required for general participation; enrollment applications may be used for related programs like trips via I&R/A or ConnectorCard via Seabury[2]
documents: No documents specified for basic participation; DC residency verification may be needed informally[6]

geography.regional_variations: Six centers across Wards 1,4,5,6,7,8 with different operators, phone numbers, hours (e.g., 8:30AM-4PM vs 8AM-5PM), websites, and event calendars; programming modes vary (e.g., Hayes in-person Tue-Thu, virtual Mon); expansions in Wards 5 and 8[2][3][5]
offices_or_providers: Ward 1: Bernice Fonteneau Senior Wellness Center, 3531 Georgia Ave NW, (202) 727-0338; Ward 5: Model Cities Senior Wellness Center, 1901 Evarts St NE, (202) 635-1900, www.seaburyresources.org/model-cities-senior-wellness-center; Ward 6: Hayes Senior Wellness Center, 500 K St NE, (202) 727-0358, www.hayesswc.com; Ward 7: Washington Seniors Wellness Center; Ward 8: Congress Heights Senior Wellness Center, 3500 MLK Jr Ave SE, (202) 563-7225[5]

gotchas: Not a Medicaid waiver program (confused with EPD Waiver which has strict income <$2,382/mo, assets <$4,000, age 65+ or disabled); no formal eligibility screening or income test—direct center contact; availability limited to specific wards/centers, not citywide; primarily drop-in wellness/social, not medical care or home assistance[1][3][5][6]
Expansions ongoing at select centers may affect capacity[2][3]
programs.data_shape_notes: Ward-specific centers with direct sign-up, no income/asset test, open to age 60+ DC residents; separate from stricter EPD Waiver program

programs.name: Legal Services for Seniors

eligibility.income_limits: No specific income limits stated for AARP Legal Counsel for the Elderly (LCE) or DACL program; prioritizes those with economic or social needs. Related programs like Legal Aid DC use 200-300% of Federal Poverty Guidelines, but not explicitly tied here[1][2][4].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in sources[1][2].
other_requirements: For LCE Legal Hotline: DC residents 60+ or 55+ with Social Security/SSI disability issues[2]
Targeted to older individuals with economic or social needs per OAA guidelines[3]

benefits.value: Free legal advice, assistance, referrals, and representation by attorneys/paralegals in: victims of abuse, medical bills, advocacy, public benefits (SS/SSI/SSDI, Medicaid, Medicare, veterans benefits), drafting wills, conservatorship/guardianship, housing/eviction/foreclosure, long-term care access, elder abuse/financial exploitation, tenant advocacy, notary services[1][2][3].

methods: Phone: DACL/DC Office on Aging (202) 724-5626 or TTY 711[1]
application.processing_time: Not specified; LCE hotline provides quick access to advice/referrals/intake[2].
forms: No specific form names/numbers listed; online intake for related Legal Aid DC[4]
documents: Documents related to legal problem if available (e.g., for upload in related online intakes)[4]

offices_or_providers: AARP Legal Counsel for the Elderly: 601 E Street, NW, Room A-4, Washington, DC 20049[1]
DC Office on Aging Notary: 500 K Street, NE, Washington, DC 20002[1]

gotchas: No explicit income/asset dollar limits for core LCE/DACL services, unlike some related programs (e.g., Legal Aid DC at 200-300% FPG)[4]; eligibility focuses on age, residency, and needs[1][2][3]
Services via hotline or specific providers; may need referral through DC Resource Bridge for broader help[4][5]
Targeted representation not guaranteed for all issues; often advice/referrals first[2]
programs.data_shape_notes: No income/asset tests specified; age/residency-based with needs priority; delivered via specific DC providers/hotlines under OAA framework[1][2][3]

programs.name: Community Transition Program

eligibility.age: 60 and older, or 18 and older with a disability[1]
eligibility.income_limits: No specific income limits stated in program materials; must have current DC Long-Term (likely Long-Term Care) services[1]
other_requirements: Have current DC Long-Term (incomplete in source, likely Long-Term Care services or Medicaid enrollment)[1]

benefits.value: Supports transition from institutional settings to community living (specific services not detailed in available sources)[1]

gotchas: Limited public details available; eligibility tied to 'current DC Long-Term' which is truncated in sources—likely requires prior Long-Term Care enrollment or assessment[1]
May overlap with Medicaid waivers or DBH transition services for mental health, but distinct program under DACL[2]
Not the same as school-based CTP or other transitional programs[4]
programs.data_shape_notes: Sparse official details; flyer cuts off key info like full eligibility and services. Administered by DACL (likely Department on Aging and Community Living). No income/asset tests explicitly stated, distinguishing from means-tested programs[1]
